title: Influence vs Pre-Suasion: Head to Head Comparison
metaDescription: Robert Cialdini's Influence vs Pre-Suasion: Which persuasion book is better? The 6 principles classic vs the timing and priming sequel compared.

book1.title: Influence
book1.subtitle: The Psychology of Persuasion
book1.author: Robert B. Cialdini
book1.description: Influence, the classic book on persuasion, explains the psychology of why people say 'yes'—and how to apply these understandings. Dr. Robert Cialdini is the seminal expert in the rapidly expanding field of influence and persuasion. His thirty-five years of rigorous, evidence-based research along with a three-year program of study on what moves people to change behavior has resulted in this highly acclaimed book.

book2.title: Pre-Suasion
book2.subtitle: A Revolutionary Way to Influence and Persuade
book2.author: Robert B. Cialdini
book2.description: When it comes to persuasion, success can begin before you say a word. In his global bestseller Influence, Professor Robert Cialdini revealed the six key principles that guide human behavior. Now, he returns with the follow-up that will change the way you think about influence. Pre-suasion is the art of arranging for recipients to be receptive to a message before they encounter it.

table.book1: The 6 principles of persuasion (what to say)
table.book2: The power of timing and context (when to say it)

table.book1: 6 weapons of influence: reciprocity, commitment, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity
table.book2: The privileged moment: priming attention before persuasion

table.book1: People use mental shortcuts (heuristics) that can be triggered
table.book2: What's focal is causal—attention determines persuasion

book1Strengths: The six principles of reciprocity, commitment, social proof, liking, authority, and scarcity explain most persuasion tactics
Real world examples from car salesmen to cult recruiters show these weapons of influence working everywhere
Click whirr automatic behavior concept explains why we fall for influence even when we know the tricks
The ethical defense framework teaches you to recognize manipulation attempts and protect yourself
book1Weaknesses: Some examples feel dated from 1980s research on compliance techniques and door to door sales
The defensive tone sometimes makes influence seem sinister rather than natural human behavior
Limited guidance on ethical use of influence when you actually need to persuade people legitimately
book2Strengths: The seven principles add unity to Cialdini's original six, explaining why we favor in group members
Pre suasion concept shows how privileged moments before asking determine whether people say yes
More business focused examples make the principles feel applicable to marketing and leadership contexts
The ethical persuasion framework addresses criticisms that influence tactics are manipulative
book2Weaknesses: Significant overlap with Influence means some content feels repetitive if you've read the original
The pre suasion concept while interesting doesn't feel as foundational as the six weapons framework
Some readers wanted completely new principles rather than refinements of existing ideas

book1: The way to love anything is to realize that it might be lost.
A well-known principle of human behavior says that when we ask someone to do us a favor we will be more successful if we provide a reason.
We are phenomenal suckers for the social proof provided by the actions of others.

Because we know that the things that are difficult to possess are typically better than those that are easy to possess, we can often use an item's availability to help us quickly and correctly decide on its quality.
Civilization advances by extending the number of operations we can perform without thinking about them.
The truly gifted negotiator is one whose initial position is exaggerated enough to allow for a series of concessions that will yield a desirable final offer from the opponent.
book2: The best persuaders become the best through pre-suasion—the process of arranging for recipients to be receptive to a message before they encounter it.
What we present first changes the way people experience what we present to them next.
When it comes to persuasion, what's focal is causal.
The factors that lead to a 'yes' are often in place before the request is ever made.
By channeling attention, we can channel choice.
The job of a persuader is not just to make a strong case for a position but also to make the audience receptive to the case.
Unity—a we-ness—creates a shared identity that causes people to act on behalf of another to a greater extent than before.

book1: Learn the 6 foundational principles that EVERY persuader uses (reciprocity, commitment, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity)
Understand why people say 'yes' with 40+ years of proven research and unforgettable stories
Essential for anyone in sales, marketing, negotiation, or leadership—universal application
Perfectly organized and immediately actionable—read once, reference forever
One of the most influential psychology books ever written (47,000 ratings at 4.7 stars)
Learn to defend yourself against unethical manipulation tactics
book2: Discover the revolutionary insight: persuasion happens BEFORE you speak (the privileged moment)
Master the art of priming attention and creating receptivity through context and timing
Learn the 7th principle (Unity) that creates shared identity and 'we-ness'
Go deeper into persuasion psychology with 30 years of additional neuroscience research
Perfect follow-up if you've already mastered Influence's 6 principles and want advanced tactics
Understand 'what's focal is causal'—how attention framing determines decisions

verdict.summary: Influence wins decisively for most readers—47,000 ratings at 4.7 vs 12,000 at 4.5, published 1984 vs 2016, and it's the foundational text with perfect organization. The 6 Principles (reciprocity, commitment, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity) are essential knowledge that everyone should learn FIRST. At 336 pages with crystal-clear structure, it's accessible and immediately actionable. Pre-Suasion is brilliant for advanced practitioners—the 'privileged moment' insight and Unity principle add depth—but it's 432 denser pages requiring Influence as prerequisite. You need to master 'what to say' (Influence) before 'when to say it' (Pre-Suasion). Influence is the textbook, Pre-Suasion is the graduate seminar.
verdict.recommendation: Read Influence FIRST—it's non-negotiable essential reading for anyone who persuades anyone (sales, marketing, parenting, teaching, managing, negotiating). Work through the 6 principles over 2-3 weeks: Chapter 2 Reciprocity (Hare Krishna donations, rejection-then-retreat), Chapter 3 Commitment (foot-in-the-door, consistency bias), Chapter 4 Social Proof (canned laughter, Jonestown, bystander effect), Chapter 5 Liking (Tupperware parties, similarity), Chapter 6 Authority (Milgram obedience), Chapter 7 Scarcity (cookie jar study, limited availability). Each principle has 40 pages of research + stories + defenses. You'll use these for life. THEN read Pre-Suasion once you've mastered Influence and want advanced depth—the privileged moment concept (prime attention before persuading) and Unity principle (shared identity) will elevate your skills. The 'what's focal is causal' insight shows how attention framing determines decisions (clouds photo = comfort focus, money photo = price focus). At 432 pages, it requires commitment, but serious persuaders need both. If you only read one: Influence, absolutely. It's the foundation with 40 years proving its value (47,000 ratings). Pre-Suasion is the advanced seminar for those who've mastered the basics. Ideal path: Influence first (2-3 weeks, practice for 6 months) → Pre-Suasion second (add advanced timing/priming tactics). Together they're complete: Influence = what to say, Pre-Suasion = when and how to set it up. Both by Robert Cialdini, both backed by decades of research, both essential for persuasion mastery.
